topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the waiting room was suffocating. Every creak of the floorboards sounded like a footstep behind me. I kept glancing at the closed door, my knuckles white where I gripped my worn backpack straps. It was supposed to be a routine intake, but the air felt thick, charged with unspoken scrutiny. When the receptionist finally called my name, a strange, almost unsettling calm washed over me, quickly replaced by a frantic need to check my reflection in the darkened window. I watched my own face, searching for any telltale sign of distress that might betray my internal turmoil. Then, the door opened, and there he was. Mark. He looked… composed. Too composed. His smile didn't quite reach his eyes, and for a split second, I felt a cold knot tighten in my stomach, convinced he was cataloging every nervous twitch.

[story 2]

I spent the entire drive home replaying every interaction from the session. Did he linger too long? Was that a subtle shift in his posture when I mentioned my anxiety about school? Every casual glance from strangers seemed loaded, an assessment being made. I kept my phone on silent, the vibration of notifications feeling like an invasion. When I finally reached my apartment, I didn't even bother turning on the lights. I just sat on the edge of my bed, listening to the distant sounds of the city, my muscles tense beneath my thin sweater. I imagined him waiting somewhere, perhaps observing, cataloging my every breath. The shadows in the corners of the room seemed to deepen, stretching into shapes that weren't quite right, confirming every suspicion I harbored about his true intentions.

[story 3]

The office smelled of expensive leather and something faintly medicinal. I stood near the reception desk, trying to appear relaxed, a performance I felt utterly incapable of executing. My hands kept fiddling with the hem of my shirt, a repetitive, anxious motion. Every time Mark made eye contact, a jolt went through me—a sudden, sharp awareness that he saw more than he let on. I noticed the way he adjusted his tie, the deliberate, slow movements that seemed calculated. *He knows,* a voice whispered in my head, cold and insistent. I started cataloging the details: the placement of the books on the shelf, the precise angle of the lamp. It was all data, evidence that he was monitoring me, that this encounter was less a consultation and more an examination.
